text.title: reporting-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for reporting-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every widget must answer "which decision does this enable?" Strip widgets without an answer.
rule.rationale: Widgets without a decision driver become status theater — pretty, ignored, never used for decisions.

rule.statement: Keep top-level dashboards to ≤7 metrics; deeper detail lives one click down.
rule.rationale: More than 7 metrics overload cognitive bandwidth and dilute attention from the critical few.

rule.statement: Tie green/yellow/red thresholds to the team's commitment, not industry averages.
rule.rationale: Generic thresholds produce misleading signals. A threshold is actionable only if the team can influence it.

rule.statement: Always show trend arrows next to absolute numbers; absolute alone is meaningless.
rule.rationale: 50 open issues is either excellent (trending down from 100) or a disaster (trending up from 25). Context matters.

rule.statement: Mobile-test executive dashboards — they get read in elevators and Ubers.
rule.rationale: Executive dashboards will be viewed on smartphones; an untestable responsive design makes them useless at decision time.

rule.statement: Version dashboard configs in git; treat schema changes like code reviews.
rule.rationale: Without version history, dashboards drift silently from reality. Git history provides the audit trail and rollback path.

rule.statement: Know your audience — tailor detail level and refresh cadence.
rule.rationale: Executives need summary + trend; teams need real-time + blockers. One dashboard for all confuses everyone.

rule.statement: Lead with insights, not data. Dashboard title should be "On Track for Q2 Release" not "Velocity = 42".
rule.rationale: Insights drive decisions; raw data requires interpretation and delays action.

rule.statement: Include recommended actions in dashboard context, not just metrics.
rule.rationale: Red status without a next step frustrates stakeholders. Always pair status with recommended action.

rule.statement: Regular cadence — build trust through consistency. Report at the same time, on the same channel, every period.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent reporting trains stakeholders to ignore it. Consistency builds trust and habit.

rule.statement: Review dashboard effectiveness quarterly: Are they read? Do they drive decisions? Retire stale dashboards.
rule.rationale: Dashboards degrade over time as business questions change. Quarterly audits prevent rot.

rule.statement: Avoid vanity metrics (velocity without context, MTTR without incident count). All metrics must drive a decision.
rule.rationale: Vanity metrics optimize teams for the metric, not the outcome, and destroy the signal-to-noise ratio.

rule.statement: Real-time refresh is rarely needed and quietly burns API quota. Refresh team dashboards hourly, management daily, executive weekly.
rule.rationale: Constant refresh adds no value past a certain cadence and multiplies data-source load.
